sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 41, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 40 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After ACTION7 #2:
- Black object: boundary [[45, 51], [45, 59], [53, 59], [53, 57]] - rows 45-53, cols 51-59
- Charcoal object: boundary [[45, 3], [45, 11], [47, 11], [47, 6]] - rows 45-53, cols 3-11
- Yellow shape horizontal bar: gone (no separate yellow horizontal bar object)
- Yellow shape vertical bar: id=10, 18 pixels, boundary [[48, 51], [48, 53], [53, 53], [53, 51]] - rows 48-53, cols 51-53
- Black object children: [7, 8, 9, 11, 12]
  - id=7: Y at (46, 52)
  - id=8: Y at (46, 55)
  - id=9: Y at (46, 58)
  - id=11: W at (49, 58)
  - id=12: W at (52, 58)
Looking at the ASCII:
```
Row 45: 'bbbbbbBBBBBBBBB'  <- black horizontal bar
Row 46: 'bbbbbbBYBBYBBYB'  <- black with yellow dots at cols 52, 55, 58
Row 47: 'bbbbbbBBBBBBBBB'  <- black horizontal bar
Row 48: 'bbbbbbYYYbbbBBB'  <- yellow vertical bar + black vertical bar
Row 49: 'bbbbbbYYYbbbBWB'  <- yellow + black with white dot
Row 50: 'bbbbbbYYYbbbBBB'  <- yellow + black
Row 51: 'bbbbbbYYYbbbBBB'  <- yellow + black
Row 52: 'bbbbbbYYYbbbBWB'  <- yellow + black with white dot
Row 53: 'bbbbbbYYYbbbBBB'  <- yellow + black
```
So ACTION7 moved the black object RIGHT by 3 columns (from cols 48-56 to cols 51-59) and the charcoal object LEFT by 3 columns (from cols 6-14 to cols 3-11).
The black object now has:
- Horizontal bar: Y at (46, 52), Y at (46, 55), Y at (46, 58)
- Vertical bar: W at (49, 58), W at (52, 58)
The yellow dots are in the horizontal bar, and the white dots are in the vertical bar. This is the opposite of the initial state where white dots were in the horizontal bar and white dots were in the vertical bar.
Wait, let me track the pattern changes:
Initial state (before any moves):
- Horizontal bar: W, W (white dots)
- Vertical bar: W, W (white dots)
After moving to overlap with yellow shape:
- Horizontal bar: W, W, Y (white, white, yellow from yellow shape)
- Vertical bar: Y, Y (yellow from yellow shape)
After ACTION7 #1:
- Horizontal bar: W, Y, Y (white, yellow, yellow)
- Vertical bar: W, W (white, white)
After ACTION7 #2:
- Horizontal bar: Y, Y, Y (yellow, yellow, yellow)
- Vertical bar: W, W (white, white)
So ACTION7 seems to shift the dots from the horizontal bar to the vertical bar. Each ACTION7 call moves one dot from horizontal to vertical (changing it from white to yellow in the horizontal bar and adding a white dot in the vertical bar).
Wait, that doesn't quite match. Let me re-examine:
- After ACTION7 #1: horizontal has 1 W, 2 Y; vertical has 2 W
- After ACTION7 #2: horizontal has 0 W, 3 Y; vertical has 2 W
So each ACTION7 call converts one white dot in the horizontal bar to a yellow dot. The vertical bar always has 2 white dots.
Hmm, but the initial state had 2 white dots in the horizontal bar and 2 white dots in the vertical bar. After overlapping with the yellow shape, the vertical bar's white dots became yellow (because they overlapped with the yellow shape). Then ACTION7 #1 restored the white dots in the vertical bar but converted one white dot in the horizontal bar to yellow. ACTION7 #2 converted the remaining white dot in the horizontal bar to yellow.
So ACTION7 seems to be "transferring" the white dots from the horizontal bar to the vertical bar. After 2 ACTION7 calls, all white dots are in the vertical bar and all dots in the horizontal bar are yellow.
Maybe the goal is to have all white dots in the vertical bar (matching the yellow shape's vertical bar pattern)? Or maybe the goal is to have a specific pattern of white/yellow dots.
Let me try pressing SPACE now that the pattern has changed, to see if it completes the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE now that ACTION7 has shifted the pattern
result = action(['SPACE'])
print("After SPACE:", result.get('board_changed'), result.get('level_completed'), result.get('done'))

# If not done, try ACTION7 again to see if it cycles
if not result.get('done'):
    result2 = action(['ACTION7'])
    print("After ACTION7 #3:", result2.get('board_changed'), result2.get('level_completed'), result2.get('done'))
    
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    print("\nBlack object children:")
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] in ['W', 'Y'] and n['pixels'] == 1:
            print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} at {n['boundary'][0]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  id=13 color=W at [52, 55]
